How records, receipts, journal storage, and offline verification compose.

Northroot is organized as a minimal trust kernel. The kernel does not need one
universal runtime to be useful.

## Architecture

```text
external runtime
  -> record JSON / JSONL
  -> canonical bytes
  -> hashes and event identity
  -> receipt bound to evidence
  -> optional append-only journal
  -> offline verification without a service dependency
```

## Implementation Notes

Domain-specific event types, authorization, reconciliation, execution lifecycle,
and semantic verification are external layers. They may consume the kernel, but
they do not become the kernel.

## Operational Lessons

Different runtimes can share evidence when the underlying records use stable
hashes, explicit receipts, and replayable journals.
A short path from source checkout to validating the first portable-evidence bundle.

The first public verification path uses the Northroot CLI and the
`github-repo-inspection` portable-evidence bundle.

## Build

```bash
cargo build --workspace
```

## Validate Primitive Files

```bash
cargo run -p northroot -- validate examples/github-repo-inspection/actor.json
cargo run -p northroot -- validate examples/github-repo-inspection/obligation.json
cargo run -p northroot -- validate examples/github-repo-inspection/policy.json
```

## Hash And Verify The Receipt

```bash
cargo run -p northroot -- hash examples/github-repo-inspection/events.jsonl
cargo run -p northroot -- verify examples/github-repo-inspection/receipt.json \
  --base-dir examples/github-repo-inspection \
  --json
```

The receipt verifier resolves `file://` evidence against the bundle directory,
hashes each evidence file, and compares the result to the receipt.

## Optional Journal Replay

The source example keeps events in JSONL. A generated `.nrj` journal is a
derived artifact for framed replay:

```bash
cargo run -p northroot -- append /tmp/github-repo-inspection.nrj /tmp/event-1.json
cargo run -p northroot -- list /tmp/github-repo-inspection.nrj --json
cargo run -p northroot -- verify /tmp/github-repo-inspection.nrj --json --strict
```

## Implementation Notes

The example intentionally starts with JSON and JSONL. The optional `.nrj`
container is reserved for cases where binary framing, streaming verification,
and truncation handling are worth the extra format.

## Operational Lessons

The quickstart should remain direct: clone, build, validate, hash, verify. The
kernel should prove evidence without requiring a hosted console.

A portable-evidence bundle for read-only repo inspection, policy-constrained events, and offline receipt verification.

The GitHub repo inspection example is the first concrete portable-evidence
bundle for Northroot primitives.

It models a read-only repository inspection where an agent checks required files
and leaves behind structured evidence. The example uses ordinary files first:
JSON for actor, obligation, policy, receipt, and manifest objects; JSONL for the
append-only event log.

## Evidence Bundle

| File | Role |
| --- | --- |
| `actor.json` | Declares the repo inspector agent and its authority scope. |
| `obligation.json` | Defines the inspection objective, constraints, and required receipts. |
| `policy.json` | Allows read access and denies write or merge actions. |
| `events.jsonl` | Records accepted obligation, tool invocation, and receipt generation events. |
| `receipt.json` | Binds the evidence hashes to a passed inspection claim. |
| `manifest.json` | Lists the bundle files and their canonical hashes. |

```bash
cargo run -p northroot -- validate examples/github-repo-inspection/obligation.json
cargo run -p northroot -- hash examples/github-repo-inspection/events.jsonl
cargo run -p northroot -- verify examples/github-repo-inspection/receipt.json \
  --base-dir examples/github-repo-inspection \
  --json
```

## What The Example Proves

- A completed work item can be described without a hosted workflow engine.
- Mutation remains explicitly denied by policy.
- Cost attribution can be recorded even when the cost is zero.
- Verification can happen from local evidence files with no live service.
- The JSONL records can be replayed into a temporary `.nrj` journal and verified.

## Implementation Notes

Runnable fixtures stay in `examples/github-repo-inspection/`. Generated `.nrj`
journals remain temporary derived artifacts; the checked-in bundle stays readable
as ordinary text.

## Operational Lessons

A useful example should leave behind evidence that can be inspected after it
leaves the runtime that produced it.
A field note on the current workspace shape: small kernel, inspectable examples, source-owned docs.

The current Northroot workspace is intentionally small.

The repository owns the public trust kernel, the first evidence example, and the
docs that explain core invariants. The site lives beside those materials as a
publication layer, not as a separate product shell.

## Current Shape

- Rust crates for canonicalization and journal verification.
- CLI commands for validation, hashing, canonicalization, event IDs, and
  receipt verification.
- JSON/JSONL examples for the first public evidence bundle.
- Static public site pages that point back to source-owned material.

## Implementation Notes

The next useful work is not a bigger homepage. It is more source-backed material:
examples, invariants, command walkthroughs, and field notes tied to source.

## Operational Lessons

Identity should grow from evidence. The site can set the direction, but the
repository has to supply the substance.
Why Northroot starts with JSON and JSONL while keeping `.nrj` as an optional export container.

Northroot starts with JSON and JSONL because they are inspectable with ordinary
tools, easy to diff, and enough for the first obligation, policy, event,
receipt, and cost examples.

The `.nrj` format remains optional. It is justified only when a workflow needs
properties that plain JSON and JSONL do not provide.

| Property | Why it matters |
| --- | --- |
| Binary framing | Avoid delimiter ambiguity in large exports. |
| Record kinds | Allow readers to skip unknown frame types. |
| Streaming verification | Verify large audit exports without loading everything. |
| Truncation handling | Distinguish strict failure from permissive partial reads. |
| Cross-language stability | Keep offline verifiers consistent across implementations. |

## Implementation Notes

If a workflow does not need those properties, it should use JSON/JSONL plus
canonical hashes. `.nrj` should stay optional until tests and examples prove the
extra format earns its keep.

## Operational Lessons

The simplest inspectable format wins until the harder format earns its keep.
What Northroot is responsible for, and what remains outside the trust kernel.

Northroot is a trust kernel for portable evidence.

It owns deterministic primitives:

- canonical JSON
- append-only records
- content-derived event identity and hashes
- receipts bound to evidence
- offline verification
- optional journal framing

It does not execute work, decide outcomes, store customer data, provide a live
operating console, or encode client-specific workflow nouns.

## Implementation Notes

The kernel stays useful because it refuses to become the runtime. Domain
systems, reconciliation layers, operating consoles, and agent frameworks can
build around it, but the core remains small enough to inspect.

## Operational Lessons

When a concern requires interpretation, policy evaluation, or action, it belongs
outside the kernel boundary.
Northroot records operational and agentic work as append-only evidence that can be copied, inspected, and verified offline.

Northroot is a trust kernel for portable evidence: append-only records,
receipts, and offline verification for operational and agentic work.

The kernel is intentionally smaller than a runtime. It gives other systems a
stable evidence shape without deciding how those systems plan, reconcile,
approve, or execute work.

## Evidence Shape

| Layer | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| Records | JSON or JSONL events describing what happened. |
| Receipts | Claims bound to local evidence hashes. |
| Journals | Optional `.nrj` containers for framed append-only replay. |
| Verification | Offline checks that do not require a Northroot service. |

## Current Proof

The first proof is the GitHub repo inspection bundle. It records a read-only
agentic work item with an actor, obligation, policy, event log, manifest, and
receipt.

```bash
cargo run -p northroot -- verify examples/github-repo-inspection/receipt.json \
  --base-dir examples/github-repo-inspection \
  --json
```

The same event log can be replayed into a temporary `.nrj` journal and verified
with the current CLI. The generated journal is a derived artifact, not the
canonical source of the example.

## Boundary

Portable evidence is not an operating console. It does not provide workflow
state, hosted orchestration, reconciliation logic, sign-in, analytics, or
approval UI. Those belong to runtimes and applications built around the kernel.

## Operational Lessons

The evidence should remain useful after it leaves the system that produced it.
That means stable hashes, explicit receipts, ordinary files first, and no hidden
service dependency for verification.
Boundary notes for what the trust kernel does not currently provide.

Northroot's public kernel is deliberately narrow. It covers evidence identity,
journal framing, and offline verification.

It does not currently provide:

- a hosted control plane
- an operating console
- an access-control product
- an agent planning system
- a workflow engine
- a customer-data store
- a policy decision runtime

Those may become separate systems later. They are not the current public kernel.

## Implementation Notes

Public pages should describe current primitives and verified examples. Internal
tools, operating consoles, and sign-in paths stay outside the public navigation
until they exist as real software.

## Operational Lessons

Trust comes from coherence and restraint. The site should show the shape of the
system without presenting future systems as current capabilities.
The site is a static publication layer, separate from source control, internal tools, and any future operating console.

The Northroot site is a static publication layer.

The first version intentionally excludes a CMS, production deploy automation,
analytics, comments, sign-in, and hosted control-plane behavior. Local Markdown
editing, local preview, and pull request review are enough.

```text
source work -> generated draft -> human edit -> merge-gated publish
```

## Implementation Notes

Generated drafts must carry source references, draft status, and a clear review
path before they can become public pages.

## Boundary Map

| Area | Role |
| --- | --- |
| Public site | Explanations, docs, examples, journal notes. |
| GitHub repo | Source of truth for code, specs, examples, and review. |
| Drafts | Editorial inputs, never automatic publication. |
| Internal tools | Out of scope for the public site until real software exists. |

## Operational Lessons

The publication layer should explain the system. It should not become the system.
Bootstrap Notes for the Northroot Site
A draft field note for the first Northroot site pass and review flow.

This area holds draft material before editorial review. Drafts are not canonical
references and should not be treated as published guidance.

## Implementation Notes

The first site pass is static and Markdown-first. It should be
easy to preview locally, review through a pull request, and delete if the
structure stops serving the work.

## Operational Lessons

- Keep source authority in the repository path that implements and tests it.
- Treat generated drafts as prompts for editorial review, not public copy.
- Prefer a narrow, stable information architecture over generic blog language.
